content.text: is the process of converting categorical (non-numeric) variables into numerical format that machine learning models can process — since models can only train on floating-point values and cannot understand strings like "dog" or "maple," we must transform categories into numerical vectors.
Categorical data represents finite discrete values

:
- Qualitative data divided into distinct groups or categories
- Examples: gender, color, product type, country, language
Gender (Male, Female), Color (Red, Blue, Green), Race, Languages
Education (High School, Bachelor's, Master's), Rating (Poor, Fair, Good, Excellent)
: Nominal variables have no order → need special encoding; Ordinal variables have order → can use ordinal encoding.
: Color variable with categories Red, Blue, Green

Before:
  Color
  Red
  Blue
  Green

After (One-Hot):
  Color_Red  Color_Blue  Color_Green
  1          0           0
  0          1           0
  0          0           1

:
- Each unique category → separate boolean column
-

(e.g., ZIP codes) → creates too many columns
- ❌

# Using scikit-learn
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

encoder = OneHotEncoder(drop='first')  # Drop first column to avoid redundancy
X_encoded = encoder.fit_transform(X)

# Using Pandas
import pandas as pd

df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['Color'], prefix=['Color'])
# Or drop first column
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df, drop_first=True)
: If you have 6 categories in "brand" column, encoder returns 6 columns. But you can drop 1 column (first) and still have all information — reduces to 5 columns without losing information.

Before:
  Color
  Red
  Blue
  Green

After (Label Encoding):
  Color
  0
  1
  2
Can work better than one-hot even without order

(misleads model)
- ❌ Example: "Good=1, Average=0, Poor=2" → artificial order not preserved
For ordinal variables where you want to preserve hierarchy:

import pandas as pd

# Define order manually
size_order = {'Small': 0, 'Medium': 1, 'Large': 2}

# Apply using replace function
df['Size_Encoded'] = df['Size'].map(size_order)

from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

encoder = LabelEncoder()
y_encoded = encoder.fit_transform(y)
: Similar to label encoding but explicitly preserves hierarchical order.
: Order is maintained in encoded values.
: Replaces category with mean of target variable for that category
: High-cardinality nominal variables (e.g., ZIP codes, product IDs)
: Reduces dimensionality, captures relationship with target
: Maps many categories to fewer dimensions using hash functions or embeddings
: Very high-cardinality features (e.g., email addresses, URLs)
: Replaces category with its frequency in dataset
The diagram below shows the decision tree for choosing the right encoding strategy given a categorical feature:

attrs.alt: Decision tree for categorical encoding: ordinal→ordinal encoding, low cardinality nominal→one-hot, high cardinality with supervision→target encoding, otherwise→hashing or frequency encoding.

content.text: Every encoding method transforms a discrete category into a real-valued vector space. The goal is that the numerical representation preserves relationships the model needs to learn while not introducing spurious relationships that do not exist in the data.
unique categories, one-hot encoding maps category
The Euclidean distance between any two one-hot vectors is always
regardless of the categories — no implicit ordering is introduced. This makes it the correct representation for nominal variables.
Label encoding maps each category to a unique integer from
This introduces an implicit ordering: the model treats
. For nominal variables this is incorrect. For ordinal variables where this order matches real-world relationships, it is appropriate.
Target encoding replaces each category with the conditional expectation of the target variable:

attrs.latex: \text{TE}(c_i) = \mathbb{E}[y \mid X = c_i] = \frac{\sum_{j: X_j = c_i} y_j}{|\{j : X_j = c_i\}|}

content.text: To prevent overfitting (categories with few samples get noisy estimates), a smoothed version blends the category mean with the global mean:
Example 1: Full Encoding Pipeline with sklearn

"""
Complete categorical encoding pipeline covering all major methods.
Dataset: customer churn prediction with mixed categorical features.
"""
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, OrdinalEncoder, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer

# Sample dataset
data = {
    "city":          ["New York", "London", "Paris", "New York", "Tokyo", "London"],
    "satisfaction":  ["Poor", "Good", "Excellent", "Good", "Poor", "Excellent"],  # ordinal
    "plan":          ["Basic", "Pro", "Enterprise", "Pro", "Basic", "Pro"],       # ordinal
    "churn":         [1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0],
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# 1. One-Hot Encoding for nominal (city — no order)
ohe = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False, drop="first")  # drop first avoids multicollinearity
city_encoded = ohe.fit_transform(df[["city"]])
print("One-Hot city columns:", ohe.get_feature_names_out(["city"]))
# ['city_London' 'city_New York' 'city_Tokyo'] — Paris dropped (baseline)

# 2. Ordinal Encoding for satisfaction (Poor < Good < Excellent)
satisfaction_order = [["Poor", "Good", "Excellent"]]
oe_satisfaction = OrdinalEncoder(categories=satisfaction_order)
sat_encoded = oe_satisfaction.fit_transform(df[["satisfaction"]])
print("Satisfaction ordinal values:", sat_encoded.flatten())
# [0. 1. 2. 1. 0. 2.]  ← Poor=0, Good=1, Excellent=2

# 3. Ordinal Encoding for plan (Basic < Pro < Enterprise)
plan_order = [["Basic", "Pro", "Enterprise"]]
oe_plan = OrdinalEncoder(categories=plan_order)
plan_encoded = oe_plan.fit_transform(df[["plan"]])

# 4. Combine with ColumnTransformer
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(transformers=[
    ("ohe",   OneHotEncoder(drop="first", sparse_output=False), ["city"]),
    ("ord_s", OrdinalEncoder(categories=[["Poor", "Good", "Excellent"]]), ["satisfaction"]),
    ("ord_p", OrdinalEncoder(categories=[["Basic", "Pro", "Enterprise"]]), ["plan"]),
])

X = preprocessor.fit_transform(df.drop("churn", axis=1))
y = df["churn"].values
print(f"Feature matrix shape: {X.shape}")  # (6, 5)
print("All encoded values:\n", X)
Example 2: Target Encoding for High-Cardinality Features

"""
Target encoding for high-cardinality categorical features.
Uses cross-validation to prevent target leakage.
"""
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

def target_encode_cv(
    train: pd.DataFrame,
    test: pd.DataFrame,
    col: str,
    target: str,
    n_folds: int = 5,
    smoothing: float = 5.0,
) -> tuple[pd.Series, pd.Series]:
    """
    Cross-validated target encoding to avoid target leakage.

    Returns encoded train and test series.
    """
    global_mean = train[target].mean()
    oof = pd.Series(index=train.index, dtype=float)

    kf = KFold(n_splits=n_folds, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
    for tr_idx, val_idx in kf.split(train):
        tr_fold = train.iloc[tr_idx]
        agg = tr_fold.groupby(col)[target].agg(["mean", "count"])
        # Smoothed encoding
        smoothed = (agg["mean"] * agg["count"] + global_mean * smoothing) / (agg["count"] + smoothing)
        oof.iloc[val_idx] = train.iloc[val_idx][col].map(smoothed).fillna(global_mean)

    # For test: encode using ALL training data
    agg_all = train.groupby(col)[target].agg(["mean", "count"])
    smoothed_all = (agg_all["mean"] * agg_all["count"] + global_mean * smoothing) / (agg_all["count"] + smoothing)
    test_encoded = test[col].map(smoothed_all).fillna(global_mean)

    return oof, test_encoded


# Example: US ZIP codes predicting house prices
np.random.seed(42)
n = 500
zip_codes  = np.random.choice([f"100{i:02d}" for i in range(50)], size=n)
prices     = 200_000 + np.random.normal(0, 30_000, n) + (zip_codes.astype(str).str[-2:].astype(int) * 1_000)

train_df = pd.DataFrame({"zip": zip_codes[:400], "price": prices[:400]})
test_df  = pd.DataFrame({"zip": zip_codes[400:], "price": prices[400:]})

tr_enc, te_enc = target_encode_cv(train_df, test_df, "zip", "price")
print(f"Train encoded sample: {tr_enc.head().values.round(0)}")
print(f"Test  encoded sample: {te_enc.head().values.round(0)}")
# Unique ZIP codes now encoded as price-signal floats — no dimensionality explosion
Real-World Example: E-Commerce Click-Through Rate Prediction
Imagine you are building a CTR (click-through rate) prediction model for an e-commerce platform. The dataset has three categorical columns:
(2,000+ brand names). Each column requires a different encoding strategy.

"""
CTR prediction: multi-strategy encoding for mixed-cardinality categoricals.
"""
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, OrdinalEncoder
from sklearn.feature_extraction import FeatureHasher
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

# Synthetic CTR dataset
np.random.seed(0)
n = 5_000
categories   = np.random.choice([f"cat_{i}" for i in range(50)], n)
devices      = np.random.choice(["mobile", "desktop", "tablet"], n,
                                 p=[0.6, 0.3, 0.1])
brands       = np.random.choice([f"brand_{i}" for i in range(2_000)], n)
click        = (np.random.rand(n) < 0.08).astype(int)  # 8% CTR

df = pd.DataFrame({"category": categories, "device": devices, "brand": brands, "click": click})
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(df, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Strategy 1: One-Hot for low cardinality device (3 values)
ohe = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False, drop="first")
device_tr = ohe.fit_transform(train_df[["device"]])
device_te = ohe.transform(test_df[["device"]])

# Strategy 2: Target encoding for medium cardinality category (50 values)
global_mean = train_df["click"].mean()
cat_agg = train_df.groupby("category")["click"].agg(["mean","count"])
smoothed = (cat_agg["mean"] * cat_agg["count"] + global_mean * 5) / (cat_agg["count"] + 5)
cat_tr = train_df["category"].map(smoothed).fillna(global_mean).values.reshape(-1,1)
cat_te = test_df["category"].map(smoothed).fillna(global_mean).values.reshape(-1,1)

# Strategy 3: Feature hashing for high cardinality brand (2000+ values → 256 dims)
hasher = FeatureHasher(n_features=256, input_type="string")
brand_tr = hasher.fit_transform([[b] for b in train_df["brand"]]).toarray()
brand_te = hasher.transform([[b] for b in test_df["brand"]]).toarray()

# Combine features
X_train = np.hstack([device_tr, cat_tr, brand_tr])
X_test  = np.hstack([device_te, cat_te, brand_te])
y_train = train_df["click"].values
y_test  = test_df["click"].values

# Train model
clf = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1])
print(f"AUC with mixed encoding strategy: {auc:.4f}")
# Typically 0.54-0.60 on synthetic random data; real datasets show larger gains
Label encoding nominal variables (e.g., city names)
Implies "Tokyo = 2 × London" — spurious ordering misleads linear models
Use OneHotEncoder for nominal features; reserve LabelEncoder for ordinal or target variables
One-hot encoding high-cardinality features (ZIP codes, user IDs)
Creates thousands of sparse columns; memory explosion and curse of dimensionality
Use target encoding, feature hashing, or embedding layers for high-cardinality
Model sees the target signal when computing the encoding → overfitting; inflated CV scores
Always compute target encoding inside cross-validation folds (fit on train, apply to val)
Fitting the encoder on the entire dataset (train+test)
Data leakage: test set category statistics pollute training; wrong evaluation
Fit on train only; transform both train and test using fit parameters
Not handling unseen categories at inference
KeyError or NaN when model encounters a category not seen during training
Use handle_unknown='ignore' in OneHotEncoder; use fillna(global_mean) for target encoding
Forgetting to drop one OHE column (dummy variable trap)
Perfect multicollinearity among one-hot columns — singular matrix in linear models
Fit encoders only on training data.
Call fit_transform on train, then transform on validation and test. Never fit on the whole dataset.
Use ColumnTransformer to apply different encodings per column.
It handles fit/transform consistently and prevents leakage.
Use KFold inside the training set to compute OOF (out-of-fold) target encodings. scikit-learn's TargetEncoder does this automatically from v1.3.
Set handle_unknown='ignore' in OneHotEncoder (returns all-zeros for unknown category) or fallback to global mean in target encoding.
Version and persist encoders alongside models.
Save the fitted encoder object (pickle/joblib) with the model artefact. Inference must use the same encoder parameters as training.
For tree-based models, check which one-hot columns have near-zero importance — they may be safe to drop, reducing dimensionality.
For deep learning, trainable embedding layers outperform one-hot encoding for medium/high cardinality features (e.g., product IDs, user IDs).
One-hot encoding for a column with
rows creates a matrix of shape
bytes ≈ 10 GB — infeasible. scikit-learn's OneHotEncoder returns a sparse CSR matrix by default, which stores only the
non-zero entries, reducing memory to ~8 MB. But downstream models that require dense input (neural networks) must convert, which negates the savings.
Feature hashing (the Hashing Trick) maps any category string to a fixed number of output dimensions via a hash function, eliminating the need to track a vocabulary. With
regardless of cardinality — and no fitting step is needed, so it works at streaming scale.

import sys, time
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_extraction import FeatureHasher

n = 100_000
k = 5_000  # 5000 unique categories

cats = np.random.choice([f"cat_{i}" for i in range(k)], n)
df_col = pd.DataFrame({"cat": cats})

# One-Hot (sparse)
ohe = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=True)
t0 = time.perf_counter()
X_ohe = ohe.fit_transform(df_col)
print(f"OneHot sparse: {time.perf_counter()-t0:.3f}s, shape {X_ohe.shape}, "
      f"memory ~{X_ohe.data.nbytes/1e6:.1f} MB (data only)")

# Feature Hashing (no fit needed, fixed 512-dim output)
hasher = FeatureHasher(n_features=512, input_type="string")
t0 = time.perf_counter()
X_hash = hasher.transform([[c] for c in cats])
print(f"FeatureHash:   {time.perf_counter()-t0:.3f}s, shape {X_hash.shape}, "
      f"memory ~{X_hash.data.nbytes/1e6:.1f} MB")
# FeatureHashing is 5-20x faster and uses fixed memory for any cardinality
categories= parameter to specify custom order; handle_unknown='use_encoded_value'
Designed for target y vector, not features; only assigns integers alphabetically
Cross-validated target encoding built into sklearn; replaces manual OOF loops
No vocabulary needed; fixed output dim; slight hash collision risk
James-Stein, WOE, leave-one-out, helmert, etc.; pip install category_encoders
Quick prototyping; not sklearn-compatible (no fit/transform); use in Jupyter exploration

Target encoding in production creates a feedback loop risk: if the target variable is noisy or changes distribution (label shift), the encoding derived from training data becomes stale and inaccurate. Retrain the encoder whenever the model is retrained; do not cache the encoding independently.

Ordinal encoding assigns integers to categories in the order they were first seen in training. If the category order changes between training runs (e.g., a new category is inserted in alphabetical position), all downstream integers shift. Always explicitly specify the
parameter with a fixed, versioned list rather than relying on auto-detection.
Save fitted OneHotEncoder/OrdinalEncoder with the model. At inference, apply the same encoder — never re-fit on inference data.
Log the rate of unknown categories at inference. A rising rate indicates concept drift in the input feature distribution.
Always compute target encoding statistics from the training split only. Use cross-validation or sklearn TargetEncoder (v1.3+) which handles this automatically.
Include the fitted encoder's categories_ attribute in the model card or registry metadata so schema changes are detectable.
: Converting categorical data to numerical vectors models can train on
: Models only train on floating-point values, not strings
: Creates binary columns (0/1) for each category, no hierarchy
: Assigns unique integer to each category, preserves order
: One-hot creates too many columns → use Target Encoding, Hashing, or Grouping
: Can use Label Encoding even without order

Encoding categoricals is the "translator" between human-readable categories and machine-readable numbers — One-Hot Encoding creates binary columns for nominal variables (no order), Label Encoding assigns integers to ordinal variables (preserves order), and advanced methods like Target Encoding or Hashing handle high-cardinality cases where simple one-hot would create too many columns, making it essential for processing qualitative data in machine learning models.
